entries.markdown: --- image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d2/Spitfire_on_Stage.jpg" image_alt: "Spitfire Live in Action" image_credit: "SpitfireMetal" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3ASpitfire_on_Stage.jpg" image_license: "CC BY-SA 4.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # Doom, Sludge, And Stoner Metal ## What it is Slow, weight-centered metal where repetition, down-tuned riffs, and environment are more important than speed. ## Canon anchors - Candlemass, Epicus Doomicus Metallicus - Eyehategod, Take as Needed for Pain - Sleep, Dopesmoker ## What succeeds - tempo used to magnify mass - repetition creating trance or dread - filthy or expansive tone with clear intent ## What fails - slow tempo with no internal motion - riff worship that never evolves ## Use it when You need pressure, ritual, or overwhelming physical drag.

entries.title: Metalcore And Modern Heavy Hybrids
entries.excerpt: image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d2/Spitfire_on_Stage.jpg" image_alt: "Spitfire Live in Action" image_credit: "<a href='//commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?title=User:SpitfireMetal&action=edit&redlink=1' class='new' title='User:SpitfireMetal (page
entries.markdown: --- image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d2/Spitfire_on_Stage.jpg" image_alt: "Spitfire Live in Action" image_credit: "SpitfireMetal" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3ASpitfire_on_Stage.jpg" image_license: "CC BY-SA 4.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # Metalcore And Modern Heavy Hybrids ## What it is Metal fused with hardcore breakdown logic, modern production, and scene-driven emotional volatility. ## Canon anchors - Converge, Jane Doe - Botch, We Are the Romans - Knocked Loose, A Different Shade of Blue ## What succeeds - breakdowns functioning as structure rather than content bait - hardcore urgency surviving polished production - emotional extremity with real hooks or rhythmic design ## What fails - hyper-edited heaviness with no personality - content-clip breakdown culture ## Use it when You need modern heavy language that balances chaos, impact, and immediacy.

entries.title: Progressive, Technical, And Atmospheric Metal
entries.excerpt: image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d2/Spitfire_on_Stage.jpg" image_alt: "Spitfire Live in Action" image_credit: "<a href='//commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?title=User:SpitfireMetal&action=edit&redlink=1' class='new' title='User:SpitfireMetal (page
entries.markdown: --- image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d2/Spitfire_on_Stage.jpg" image_alt: "Spitfire Live in Action" image_credit: "SpitfireMetal" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3ASpitfire_on_Stage.jpg" image_license: "CC BY-SA 4.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # Progressive, Technical, And Atmospheric Metal ## What it is Metal that imports odd meter, advanced harmony, extreme precision, or wide-screen texture. ## Canon anchors - Meshuggah, Destroy Erase Improve - Opeth, Blackwater Park - Gorguts, Obscura ## What succeeds - complexity still serving heaviness or atmosphere - structure that rewards close listening - technique integrated with identity ## What fails - musicianship as self-justification - clean technical production without menace ## Use it when You need metal to think architecturally rather than only pound forward.

entries.title: Thrash, Speed, And Crossover Metal
entries.excerpt: image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d2/Spitfire_on_Stage.jpg" image_alt: "Spitfire Live in Action" image_credit: "<a href='//commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?title=User:SpitfireMetal&action=edit&redlink=1' class='new' title='User:SpitfireMetal (page
entries.markdown: --- image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d2/Spitfire_on_Stage.jpg" image_alt: "Spitfire Live in Action" image_credit: "SpitfireMetal" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3ASpitfire_on_Stage.jpg" image_license: "CC BY-SA 4.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # Thrash, Speed, And Crossover Metal ## What it is Fast, riff-dense metal that fuses punk aggression with precise ensemble attack. ## Canon anchors - Metallica, Ride the Lightning - Slayer, Reign in Blood - Sepultura, Beneath the Remains ## What succeeds - speed guided by arrangement clarity - rhythm-guitar precision creating tension - anger with structural shape ## What fails - velocity without memorable riffs - one-level intensity across the whole record ## Use it when You want heavy music that feels urgent, political, or militarized.

entries.title: Traditional Heavy Metal And NWOBHM
entries.excerpt: image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d2/Spitfire_on_Stage.jpg" image_alt: "Spitfire Live in Action" image_credit: "<a href='//commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?title=User:SpitfireMetal&action=edit&redlink=1' class='new' title='User:SpitfireMetal (page
entries.markdown: --- image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d2/Spitfire_on_Stage.jpg" image_alt: "Spitfire Live in Action" image_credit: "SpitfireMetal" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3ASpitfire_on_Stage.jpg" image_license: "CC BY-SA 4.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # Traditional Heavy Metal And NWOBHM ## What it is Riff-driven metal built on gallop rhythm, dramatic vocals, and sharpened hard-rock power. ## Canon anchors - Black Sabbath, Master of Reality - Judas Priest, Screaming for Vengeance - Iron Maiden, The Number of the Beast ## What succeeds - riffs and choruses both carrying the record - theatricality backed by true momentum - guitar tone serving melody and attack ## What fails - retro worship with no danger - leather-and-studs image replacing songwriting ## Use it when You need heavy music with clarity, propulsion, and anthem potential.

headings.text: Symphonic prog and classic progressive rock
Math rock and later progressive mutation

entries.markdown:
--- image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/ac/Hawklords_live_at_HRH_Prog_2025_in_Gt_Yarmouth_%2854961369804%29.jpg" image_alt: "Hawklords put in a typically high-energy psychedelic stoner space rock performance in their afternoon slot at HRH Prog.

Jerry Richards - guitar, vocals, SFX Dave Pearce - drums, percussion Kriss Gordelier - vocals

Graham Manson - bass" image_credit: "Steve Knight" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3AHawklords_live_at_HRH_Prog_2025_in_Gt_Yarmouth_(54961369804).jpg" image_license: "CC BY 4.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # Progressive And Psychedelic Music ## Core identity This family exists wherever rock, jazz, electronic, or folk structures are stretched toward altered perception, long-form design, conceptual sequencing, and timbral adventure. It matters because many ambitious alternative records still inherit its appetite for scale and texture. ## Main subgenres ### Psychedelic rock and acid folk The Beatles' later work, Pink Floyd's early period, Love, The 13th Floor Elevators, Incredible String Band, and related scenes use studio manipulation, modal drift, and surreal lyric space to destabilize normal song perception. It works when altered-state logic produces memorable forms. ### Symphonic prog and classic progressive rock King Crimson, Yes, Genesis, Van der Graaf Generator, and later progressive bands turn albums into suites, combine virtuosity with dynamic contrast, and treat arrangement as world-building. Communities reward the branch when drama justifies complexity. ### Krautrock and motorik modernism Can, Neu!, Faust, Harmonia, Popol Vuh, and related German scenes reject Anglo blues-rock assumptions in favor of repetition, studio experimentation, trance pulse, and new machine-human identities. This branch remains hugely influential across post-punk, electronic music, and indie experimentation. ### Canterbury, jazz-prog, and chamber-progressive branches Soft Machine, Henry Cow, National Health, Univers Zero, and related artists fuse rock instrumentation with jazz harmony, chamber texture, or absurdist compositional thinking. It matters because prog can be playful and strange rather than only grandiose. ### Post-rock and instrumental expansion Talk Talk's late work, Slint, Tortoise, Godspeed You! Black Emperor, Sigur Ros, and others use rock instrumentation for cinematic buildup, repetition, and textural composition rather than verse-chorus drive. The enduring work turns patience into revelation. ### Math rock and later progressive mutation Don Caballero, Battles, black midi-adjacent complexity, and other later branches fuse rhythmic difficulty, precision, and tonal experimentation. This works when the listener still feels suspense or humor. It fails when oddity becomes mere puzzle-solving. ## What communities keep rewarding - ambition with audible stakes - timbral exploration tied to form - albums that feel sequenced rather than shuffled - complexity that produces wonder, dread, or trance ## What usually fails - virtuosity without drama - concept bloat - faux-cosmic atmosphere with weak melodies - endless build with no arrival ## Why this family still matters Progressive and psychedelic music remain central whenever artists want to push beyond functional song craft into total-environment listening.

--- image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/ac/Hawklords_live_at_HRH_Prog_2025_in_Gt_Yarmouth_%2854961369804%29.jpg" image_alt: "Hawklords put in a typically high-energy psychedelic stoner space rock performance in their afternoon slot at HRH Prog.

Jerry Richards - guitar, vocals, SFX Dave Pearce - drums, percussion Kriss Gordelier - vocals

Graham Manson - bass" image_credit: "Steve Knight" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3AHawklords_live_at_HRH_Prog_2025_in_Gt_Yarmouth_(54961369804).jpg" image_license: "CC BY 4.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # Canterbury And Jazz-Prog ## What it is Progressive music using jazz harmony, chamber texture, and often sly or absurd tonal character. ## Canon anchors - Soft Machine, Third - Henry Cow, Unrest - National Health, Of Queues and Cures ## What succeeds - complexity with wit or curiosity - instrumental writing that feels conversational - odd harmony with real movement ## What fails - fussy arrangement without emotional center - prog cleverness becoming self-congratulation ## Use it when You need articulated ensemble writing outside blunt rock tropes.

Jerry Richards - guitar, vocals, SFX Dave Pearce - drums, percussion Kriss Gordelier - vocals

Graham Manson - bass" image_credit: "Steve Knight" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3AHawklords_live_at_HRH_Prog_2025_in_Gt_Yarmouth_(54961369804).jpg" image_license: "CC BY 4.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # Krautrock And Motorik Modernism ## What it is German experimental rock emphasizing repetition, studio process, motorik pulse, and anti-blues modernism. ## Canon anchors - Can, Future Days - Neu!, Neu! 75 - Faust, Faust IV ## What succeeds - trance repetition with textural mutation - refusal of inherited rock cliché - studio experimentation that feels purposeful ## What fails - motorik imitation with no personality - “cosmic” drift without compositional pressure ## Use it when You need forward motion, machine-human ambiguity, or post-rock modernism.

entries.excerpt:

--- image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/ac/Hawklords_live_at_HRH_Prog_2025_in_Gt_Yarmouth_%2854961369804%29.jpg" image_alt: "Hawklords put in a typically high-energy psychedelic stoner space rock performance in their afternoon slot at HRH Prog.

Jerry Richards - guitar, vocals, SFX Dave Pearce - drums, percussion Kriss Gordelier - vocals

Graham Manson - bass" image_credit: "Steve Knight" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3AHawklords_live_at_HRH_Prog_2025_in_Gt_Yarmouth_(54961369804).jpg" image_license: "CC BY 4.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # Math Rock And Later Progressive Mutation ## What it is Rhythmically intricate rock that values angular riffs, sudden turns, and compositional precision. ## Canon anchors - Don Caballero, What Burns Never Returns - Battles, Mirrored - black midi, Schlagenheim ## What succeeds - complexity creating suspense or absurd energy - precision without emotional deadness - tonal or rhythmic ideas clear enough to follow ## What fails - puzzle music with no atmosphere or stakes - virtuosity so clean it loses friction ## Use it when You want modern progressive language with high rhythmic tension and anti-classic-rock posture.

Black Emperor, Lift Your Skinny Fists Like Antennas to Heaven ## What succeeds - long-form patience leading to real arrival - texture and dynamics telling the story - instrumental music that still feels narratively loaded ## What fails - endless crescendo formula - atmosphere without event or consequence ## Use it when You want rock-band resources to function like composition and landscape.

image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0"
image_usage: "permissive"

# Symphonic Progressive Rock

## What it is

Album-scale rock that uses suite form, virtuosity, and orchestral ambition.

## Canon anchors

- King Crimson, In the Court of the Crimson King
- Yes, Close to the Edge
- Genesis, Selling England by the Pound

## What succeeds

- complexity tied to dramatic progression
- strong themes returning in transformed ways
- grandeur with actual tension

## What fails

- bloated solos and concept sprawl
- technical display without narrative payoff

## Use it when

You need big-form architecture and theatrical sequencing.

headings.text: New wave, synth-pop, and pop-facing mutation
No wave, dance-punk, and later angular revivals

entries.markdown:
--- image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/aa/La_Chatte_-_exposition_Crash_Ocean%2C_Le_Confort_Moderne%2C_Poitiers_%282012-01-18_21.34.59_by_Xi_WEG%29.jpg" image_alt: "

Flickr Tags: Confort Moderne Confort Moderne Poitiers Concert Music Musique Musicians Musiciens Stage Live Playing Scène Vava Dudu Exposition Entrepot Galerie Crash Ocean post-punk electro-zouk Performance Nikolu Stereovoid Stéphane Argillet Nicolas Jorio.

from Flickr album 'Le Confort Moderne' by Xi WEG from Poitiers, France" image_credit: "Xi WEG from Poitiers, France" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3ALa_Chatte_-_exposition_Crash_Ocean%2C_Le_Confort_Moderne%2C_Poitiers_(2012-01-18_21.34.59_by_Xi_WEG).jpg" image_license: "CC BY 2.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # Punk, Post-Punk, And New Wave ## Core identity This family is built from reduction, speed, irony, confrontation, and scene self-definition. It matters because it repeatedly resets bloated musical ecosystems and then mutates into more durable forms of artful tension, style, and political unease. ## Main subgenres ### First-wave punk and street-level reduction Ramones, Sex Pistols, The Clash, Buzzcocks, and many local scenes strip rock down to speed, hooks, and antagonistic clarity. The enduring records still sound alive because simplicity is used as force, not as laziness. ### Hardcore punk and post-hardcore Black Flag, Minor Threat, Bad Brains, Fugazi, Husker Du, and many later descendants make punk faster, more disciplined, and more ethically or structurally self-conscious. This branch matters because DIY infrastructure and musical seriousness finally fuse. ### Post-punk and art-damaged modernism Joy Division, Wire, Public Image Ltd., The Fall, Gang of Four, Talking Heads, and many others keep punk's refusal while importing dub, funk, electronics, and avant-garde tension. This is one of the central sources of alternative music after 1978. ### Goth, darkwave, and cold atmosphere Siouxsie and the Banshees, Bauhaus, The Cure, Cocteau Twins, Dead Can Dance, and related scenes transform post-punk into spectral mood, ritual elegance, and emotional stylization. Communities reward this branch when atmosphere and songwriting are both strong. ### New wave, synth-pop, and pop-facing mutation Blondie, Devo, The Cars, New Order, Pet Shop Boys, and many others smooth punk energy into clever, danceable, media-aware pop. It works when hooks and design intelligence reinforce each other instead of collapsing into novelty. ### No wave, dance-punk, and later angular revivals No wave, ESG, Liquid Liquid, LCD Soundsystem, and later dance-punk or art-punk revivals keep the family restless and rhythmically sharp. This branch matters because tension, groove, and urban abrasion can coexist. ## What communities keep rewarding - economy with purpose - scene identity backed by real sonic choices - tension between rawness and design - records that sound necessary rather than merely cool ## What usually fails - cosplay rebellion - retro punk gesture with no present-tense target - ironic detachment covering weak writing - monochrome aggression with no rhythmic intelligence ## Why this family still matters Punk and its mutations survive because they are still the quickest way to expose dead convention and force decisions.

A few days before embarking on La
entries.markdown:
--- image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/4/40/Katy_Goodman%2C_La_Sera%2C_Room_205%2C_2010-11-16.jpg" image_alt: "
  • Katy Goodman

A few days before embarking on La Sera's first UK tour, mastermind Katy Goodman (vox/bass) and hired guns Jennifer Prince (guitar/vox) and Jonathan Weinberg (drums/dry humor), stopped by Room 205 to blast through a few songs from Katy's upcoming full-length on Hardly Art.

BIO

Since joining all-female punk trio Vivian Girls during the spring of 2007, vocalist/bassist Katy Goodman's dreamy DIY talents have sent waves of admiration throughout the independent music community. After touring the world, releasing a handful of well-received records, and starting the label Wild World with her Vivian Girls band mates, Katy played in the short-lived dream-pop band All Saints Day and released a hard-to-find self-titled 7' of spectral pop tunes. In February 2010 she began working on fresh material for her new side band, La Sera. According to Goodman, the project's inspiration springs from an attraction to ethereal choral vocals and pop hits of the 1950's. Indeed.

" image_credit: "Incase from San Francisco, USA" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3AKaty_Goodman%2C_La_Sera%2C_Room_205%2C_2010-11-16.jpg" image_license: "CC BY 2.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # First-Wave Punk ## What it is Fast, hooky, antagonistic rock stripped down to speed, attitude, and social abrasion. ## Canon anchors - Ramones, Rocket to Russia - Sex Pistols, Never Mind the Bollocks, Here's the Sex Pistols - The Clash, London Calling ## What succeeds - economy with real bite - songs short enough to hit immediately but distinct enough to last - anger focused on a target ## What fails - rebel costume without present-tense grievance - simplistic playing with no rhythmic spark ## Use it when You need direct confrontation, speed, and anti-bloat clarity.

from Flickr album 'Le Confort Moderne' by Xi WEG from Poitiers, France" image_credit: "Xi WEG from Poitiers, France" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3ALa_Chatte_-_exposition_Crash_Ocean%2C_Le_Confort_Moderne%2C_Poitiers_(2012-01-18_21.34.59_by_Xi_WEG).jpg" image_license: "CC BY 2.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # Goth And Darkwave ## What it is Atmospheric descendants of post-punk emphasizing ritual, shadow, romantic dread, and sculpted mood. ## Canon anchors - Bauhaus, In the Flat Field - The Cure, Pornography - Cocteau Twins, Treasure ## What succeeds - atmosphere backed by unforgettable melody or texture - drama that feels seductive rather than camp by default - rhythm sections keeping the darkness in motion ## What fails - costume gothic with generic minor-key writing - mood fixation with no song strength ## Use it when You need spectral beauty, ritual tension, or nocturnal elegance.

from Flickr album 'Le Confort Moderne' by Xi WEG from Poitiers, France" image_credit: "Xi WEG from Poitiers, France" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3ALa_Chatte_-_exposition_Crash_Ocean%2C_Le_Confort_Moderne%2C_Poitiers_(2012-01-18_21.34.59_by_Xi_WEG).jpg" image_license: "CC BY 2.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # New Wave And Synth-Pop ## What it is Pop-facing mutation of punk and post-punk using hooks, electronics, style intelligence, and media awareness. ## Canon anchors - Devo, Q: Are We Not Men? A: We Are Devo! - New Order, Power, Corruption and Lies - Pet Shop Boys, Actually ## What succeeds - melody and design equally strong - synthetic sound used with wit and emotional clarity - pop economy without flattening personality ## What fails - novelty songwriting - retro synth gloss with no hook authority ## Use it when You need sharp pop form with ironic or elegant modern surfaces.

entries.title: No Wave And Dance-Punk
entries.excerpt: image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/2/21/Shellac-band.jpg" image_alt: "The American punk rock band Shellac in Shibuya, Tokyo, Japan. Front to back:
entries.markdown:
--- image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/2/21/Shellac-band.jpg" image_alt: "The American punk rock band Shellac in Shibuya, Tokyo, Japan. Front to back: Bob Weston, Steve Albini, Todd Trainer. Though the photo is undated, it was almost certainly taken around the time when Shellac played their first concert in Japan at Yoyogi Chocolate City on November 21, 1993, as it was the band's only concert in Japan before the photo was uploaded to Flickr in 2006; the live recording of that performance was released in Japan in 1994 as Live in Tokyo (ライヴイン東京).

Original caption via Flickr:

Steve Albini's band Shellac

Albini is a very famous producer(NIrvana.etc)

Shellac is a his own band after Big Black broke up.

this photo took at Shibuya.

" image_credit: "Masao Nakagami" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3AShellac-band.jpg" image_license: "CC BY-SA 2.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # No Wave And Dance-Punk ## What it is Rhythm-forward, abrasive music where funk motion, punk refusal, and urban edge stay in conflict. ## Canon anchors - ESG, Come Away with ESG - Liquid Liquid, Optimo - LCD Soundsystem, Sound of Silver ## What succeeds - tension and groove coexisting - abrasion sharpened by repetition - bands sounding physically locked-in, not merely stylish ## What fails - posturing coolness with weak rhythm - dance-punk copied as a surface package ## Use it when You need angular groove and social tension in the same frame.

500px provided description: Benjamin Turner Damerels [#show ,#night ,#light ,#rock ,#lights ,#punk ,#uk ,#england ,#concert ,#music ,#live ,#performance ,#stage ,#gig ,#band ,#musician ,#drums ,#drummer]

entries.title: Reggae, Dub, And Ska
entries.excerpt: image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/61/Lad%C3%A1nybene_27%2C_2011_%281%29.jpg" image_alt: "Live at Downtown Budapest, on the 4th of September, 2011. Ladánybene 27 band has begun to play reggae after listening to Bob Marley's Babylon by Bus concert album. Firs
entries.markdown:
--- image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/61/Lad%C3%A1nybene_27%2C_2011_%281%29.jpg" image_alt: "Live at Downtown Budapest, on the 4th of September, 2011. Ladánybene 27 band has begun to play reggae after listening to Bob Marley's Babylon by Bus concert album. First, they toured with a program of ska songs, later roots reggae had more and more emphasis on their music that is best shown on the first album by the song called 'Rastafari'. This first album was a revolutionary event in 1991 since nobody had released a reggae album before in Hungary. That's how the band won the eMeRton prize of the Hungarian Radio Stations. The success of the second album can be connected with the song called 'Keep on Picking' (Szedegetem) which became the first Hungarian reggae hit and stayed on the top of the lists for weeks. The band first played Bob Marley's songs on the following 'Live' album that was recorded on a charity concert with 3000 people. For the fiftieth anniversary of Bob Marley's birth, the band released an album with their favourite Bob Marley songs. Before the fourth album we toured in Jamaica and recorded two videoclips and made a short film about the journey. As a result of the live appearancesin the heartland of reggae, LB 27 was invited to play on the prestigious SUNSPLASH and SUMFEST festivals. For the fiftieth anniversary of Bob Marley's birth, the band released an album with their favourite Bob Marley songs. The 6th album of LB 27 came out in November 1996 titled Pozitív' (Positive) and released by EMI-Quint. 'Kell egy ház' is the title of the band's 7th album. This old Natural Ites song (Picture on the Wall) has become the best known reggae song in Hungary ever thus their earlier publisher released a 'best of' album with songs from their first four albums. LB27 continuously makes efforts to make reggae well-known in Hungary since in the 60'-70'-80's, the local radio stations simply ignored reggae music thus it stayed almost completely unknown untill these days. Except for Marley's best known songs, they played only the disco-effected hits, that's why LB 27 plays several different styles such as: roots, dub, dancehall, ragga, ska. On the concerts, summer festivals, university organizations, thousands of people dance to their music, not mentiomimg those club programs(Jammin' Reggae Party) where they play songs form Alpha Blondy to The Wailers as LB Reggae Sound System in a DJ program. The first Hungarian internet radio (http://www.para.c3.hu/paraggae), the ParaGgae worths mentioning as well, where on Wednesdays you can listen to reggae music chosen by the band. One of the band's most memorable events was the Carlos Santana concert in Budapest 1996, where the band could play Bob Marley's Exodus together with Santana and his band. On an other occassion, Miksa could sing 'Get up, Stand up' together with Pato Banton on the Malibu Reggae Mix in the same year. The band gives concerts both in Hungary and abroad thus they could take part in Rototom Sunsplash last year. Mr. Rae Barrett took notice of the band and invited them to Jamaica where this February in Ocho Rios LB27 and The Wailers played 'One Love' together on the Bob Marley birthday concert.

Members: Tibi 'T.B.Homelock' Bodnár (The Boss, guitar vocal), Miksa M. László (lead vocal),

Tamás 'Rastamás' Árokszállási (drums), István 'Rasta' Hertelendi (bass), Zoltán 'Apu' Bokó (keyboards), Levente 'Lewy Brandy' Pálinkás (percussion, keyboards, vocal). Címke: Ladánybene 27. Budapest Metapolisz Budapest Szive Program LB 27. Doktor kérlek. Bob Marley jamaica reggae rasta jamaican music Árokszállási Ras Tamás. Published under Creative Commons in the Metapolisz DVD line." image_credit: "Derzsi Elekes Andor" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3ALad%C3%A1nybene_27%2C_2011_(1).jpg" image_license: "CC BY-SA 3.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # Reggae, Dub, And Ska ## Core identity This family is built around offbeat emphasis, bass gravity, studio space, and the circulation of rhythm through sound-system culture. It matters because it changes the role of bass, echo, and groove in global music more radically than many better-advertised rock movements. ## Main subgenres ### Ska and rocksteady The Skatalites, Prince Buster, Desmond Dekker, and early Jamaican scenes establish upstroke rhythm, horn-led arrangement, and dance-social immediacy. Rocksteady slows the pulse and deepens vocal emotion, setting the stage for reggae's bass-centric future. ### Roots reggae Bob Marley and the Wailers, Burning Spear, Culture, The Abyssinians, and many others fuse bass, groove, anti-colonial thought, Rasta spirituality, and ensemble economy. Communities still reward roots reggae when the groove feels spacious but morally charged. ### Dub King Tubby, Lee Perry, Augustus Pablo, Scientist, and later dub innovators turn the mixing desk into an instrument. This is one of the most important genre branches in all of production history. Delay, reverb, subtraction, and bass become composition. ### Dancehall and ragga Yellowman, Shabba Ranks, Beenie Man, and later dancehall scenes intensify digital rhythm, vocal charisma, and sound-system competition. The strongest work sounds local, physical, and immediate. The weakest work chases shock without rhythmic depth. ### Dub diaspora and bass continuation Post-punk dub, trip-hop, jungle, dub techno, UK bass culture, and many contemporary hybrids prove how widely Jamaican production logic spread. This branch matters because so much “modern” production still runs on dub ideas whether it admits it or not. ## What communities keep rewarding - bass treated as lead narrative force - negative space with real tension - vocal charisma against deep rhythm - studio intervention that increases mystery rather than clutter ## What usually fails - tourist reggae pastiche - groove flattened by over-quantized production - dub effects sprayed over weak arrangements - political or spiritual language used only as exotic texture ## Why this family still matters Reggae and dub remain essential because they permanently changed how producers think about bass, space, and the mix itself.

Members: Tibi 'T.B.Homelock' Bodnár (The Boss, guitar vocal), Miksa M. László (lead vocal),

Tamás 'Rastamás' Árokszállási (drums), István 'Rasta' Hertelendi (bass), Zoltán 'Apu' Bokó (keyboards), Levente 'Lewy Brandy' Pálinkás (percussion, keyboards, vocal). Címke: Ladánybene 27. Budapest Metapolisz Budapest Szive Program LB 27. Doktor kérlek. Bob Marley jamaica reggae rasta jamaican music Árokszállási Ras Tamás. Published under Creative Commons in the Metapolisz DVD line." image_credit: "Derzsi Elekes Andor" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3ALad%C3%A1nybene_27%2C_2011_(1).jpg" image_license: "CC BY-SA 3.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # Dancehall And Ragga ## What it is Digitally driven Jamaican rhythm music centered on vocal charisma, sound-system competition, and hard rhythmic economy. ## Canon anchors - Yellowman, Mister Yellowman - Shabba Ranks, X-tra Naked - Vybz Kartel, Kingston Story ## What succeeds - rhythm tracks stripped to the right essentials - voice carrying swagger, wit, and threat - immediacy without losing groove depth ## What fails - shock-value writing over generic riddims - digital production that removes physical heft ## Use it when You need raw rhythmic directness and vocal-command energy.

entries.title: Dub
entries.excerpt: image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d1/Pat_Flashman.jpg" image_alt: "Pat Flashman is one of the pioneers of reggae-music in Belgium. In 1977 Pat and Ras Feel built the first Belgian reggae sound system, King Flashman Elektro. In the 80s Pat was the drummer of
entries.markdown: --- image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d1/Pat_Flashman.jpg" image_alt: "Pat Flashman is one of the pioneers of reggae-music in Belgium. In 1977 Pat and Ras Feel built the first Belgian reggae sound system, King Flashman Elektro. In the 80s Pat was the drummer of King Flashman's Live Reggae Band. From 1988 until 1994 Pat played the drums for Crucial Crew. Nowadays Pat makes dub-music with his computer, using samples and sometimes live musicians." image_credit: "Dubrider" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3APat_Flashman.jpg" image_license: "CC BY-SA 4.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # Dub ## What it is Mix-engineered music where echo, reverb, subtraction, and bass redesign the original material into a new composition. ## Canon anchors - King Tubby, King Tubby Meets Rockers Uptown - Lee Perry, Blackboard Jungle Dub - Scientist, Scientist Rids the World of the Evil Curse of the Vampires ## What succeeds - space used compositionally - dropouts and effects timed like arrangement events - bass and drum focus creating altered-state depth ## What fails - delay spam over weak tracks - effects used decoratively rather than structurally ## Use it when You need production itself to become form and atmosphere.

entries.title: Dub Diaspora And Bass Continuations
entries.excerpt: image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/6e/Sound_System.jpg" image_alt: "Dub sound-system crew preparing a speaker stack for performance" image_credit: "Dubdem e FabDub" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3ASound_System.jpg" image_license:
entries.markdown:
---
image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/6e/Sound_System.jpg"
image_alt: "Dub sound-system crew preparing a speaker stack for performance"
image_credit: "Dubdem e FabDub"
image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3ASound_System.jpg"
image_license: "CC BY 2.0"
image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0"
image_usage: "permissive"
---

# Dub Diaspora And Bass Continuations

## What it is

Dub logic extended into post-punk, trip-hop, jungle, dub techno, and modern bass music.

## Canon anchors

- The Slits, Cut
- Basic Channel, BCD
- Burial, Untrue

## What succeeds

- keeping dub's sense of negative space and bass priority
- adaptation without flattening Jamaican rhythmic intelligence
- texture reinforcing groove rather than replacing it

## What fails

- token echo and sub-bass used as trend markers
- genre fusion with no pocket

## Use it when

You want dub's production philosophy inside non-reggae forms.

Tamás 'Rastamás' Árokszállási (drums), István 'Rasta' Hertelendi (bass), Zoltán 'Apu' Bokó (keyboards), Levente 'Lewy Brandy' Pálinkás (percussion, keyboards, vocal). Címke: Ladánybene 27. Budapest Metapolisz Budapest Szive Program LB 27. Doktor kérlek. Bob Marley jamaica reggae rasta jamaican music Árokszállási Ras Tamás. Published under Creative Commons in the Metapolisz DVD line." image_credit: "Derzsi Elekes Andor" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3ALad%C3%A1nybene_27%2C_2011_(1).jpg" image_license: "CC BY-SA 3.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # Roots Reggae ## What it is Bass-heavy Jamaican music where groove, spirituality, anti-colonial thought, and vocal directness meet. ## Canon anchors - Bob Marley and the Wailers, Exodus - Burning Spear, Marcus Garvey - The Abyssinians, Satta Massagana ## What succeeds - deep pocket with lots of air - lyrical gravity matched by arrangement restraint - bass carrying both melody and worldview ## What fails - postcard reggae feel stripped of its political core - groove flattened by modern quantization ## Use it when You need meditative but insistent rhythm and moral weight.

entries.title: Ska And Rocksteady
entries.excerpt: image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/2/25/Roots_Family_At_Suoni_Di_Marca_Festival_Treviso_%2843933504%29.jpeg" image_alt: "<div class='description'> 500px provided description: Roots Family live exhibition 11/08/2013 [#outdoor ,#concert ,#outdoors ,#tour ,#event

--- image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/2/25/Roots_Family_At_Suoni_Di_Marca_Festival_Treviso_%2843933504%29.jpeg" image_alt: "
500px provided description: Roots Family live exhibition 11/08/2013 [#outdoor ,#concert ,#outdoors ,#tour ,#event ,#live performance ,#live show ,#concerto ,#all'aperto ,#esibizione ,#luogo ,#public performance ,#tourn?e ,#esecuzione dal vivo ,#evento musicale ,#manifestazione musicale ,#musical entertainment ,#tipo esibizione ,#tourn? ,#Roots Reggae music]
" image_credit: "
Marco Bertuzzo
" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3ARoots_Family_At_Suoni_Di_Marca_Festival_Treviso_(43933504).jpeg" image_license: "CC BY 3.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # Ska And Rocksteady ## What it is Early Jamaican popular music built on offbeat guitar or piano accents, vocal harmonies, and dance-ready momentum. ## Canon anchors - The Skatalites, Ska Authentic - Desmond Dekker, Israelites-era recordings - Alton Ellis, rocksteady classics ## What succeeds - offbeat precision without stiffness - brass or vocal arrangement lifting the rhythm section - lightness carrying real drive ## What fails - novelty-party ska reduction - upbeat surface with no bass discipline ## Use it when You need buoyant rhythm and sharp upstroke energy.

entries.title: Rock
entries.excerpt: image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/6f/Velvet_Crest_By_Popular_Demand_Band_Live_Performance_Mineral_City_Ohio_c._1968.jpg" image_alt: "Velvet Crest, a 1960s garage rock band from Mineral City, Ohio originally known as By Popular Demand, perform at a concert c

headings.text: British invasion, garage rock, and proto-punk
Hard rock, glam, and arena-scale rock
Album rock, heartland rock, and adult rock realism
Garage revival and later rock renewal

entries.markdown: --- image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/6f/Velvet_Crest_By_Popular_Demand_Band_Live_Performance_Mineral_City_Ohio_c._1968.jpg" image_alt: "Velvet Crest, a 1960s garage rock band from Mineral City, Ohio originally known as By Popular Demand, perform at a concert c. 1968." image_credit: "Imokurnotok" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3AVelvet_Crest_By_Popular_Demand_Band_Live_Performance_Mineral_City_Ohio_c._1968.jpg" image_license: "CC BY-SA 4.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # Rock ## Core identity Rock is amplified ensemble music built around riff, backbeat, bodily force, and the belief that a band can represent a worldview. It matters because even artists reacting against rock often still inherit its ideas about intensity, album identity, and public stance. ## Main subgenres ### Rock and roll and rockabilly Chuck Berry, Little Richard, Elvis Presley, Carl Perkins, and early architects fuse blues, country, rhythm and blues, and youth energy into concise, body-first records. This branch matters because attitude and hook density are inseparable from the form. ### British invasion, garage rock, and proto-punk The Beatles, The Rolling Stones, The Kinks, The Who, The Sonics, MC5, and The Stooges push rock toward louder identity, sharper riffs, and ruder textures. It works when urgency and songcraft reinforce each other. ### Hard rock, glam, and arena-scale rock Jimi Hendrix, Led Zeppelin, AC/DC, Queen, Thin Lizzy, and later arena acts prove that scale can still feel dangerous if the riffs, grooves, and drama are strong. The failed version is simply big-budget empty confidence. ### Album rock, heartland rock, and adult rock realism Neil Young, Bruce Springsteen, Tom Petty, Patti Smith, and related artists use rock to address adulthood, work, place, and public life. This branch matters because rock does not always have to be adolescent spectacle. ### Garage revival and later rock renewal The White Stripes, The Strokes, Yeah Yeah Yeahs, Japandroids, and other revivalists keep returning to stripped gear, sharp hooks, and live-wire energy. Communities reward these revivals when they sound necessary rather than archivist. ## What communities keep rewarding - riff writing with real memorability - ensemble chemistry that feels bigger than individuals - loudness tied to social or emotional meaning - albums with a clear stance toward life ## What usually fails - classic-rock worship without present-tense stakes - macho cliche mistaken for conviction - overcompressed radio rock with no dynamic arc - loudness used to hide weak songs ## Why this family still matters Rock remains useful whenever a project needs collective force, confrontation, and physical arrangement logic.

entries.markdown: --- image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/6f/Velvet_Crest_By_Popular_Demand_Band_Live_Performance_Mineral_City_Ohio_c._1968.jpg" image_alt: "Velvet Crest, a 1960s garage rock band from Mineral City, Ohio originally known as By Popular Demand, perform at a concert c. 1968." image_credit: "Imokurnotok" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3AVelvet_Crest_By_Popular_Demand_Band_Live_Performance_Mineral_City_Ohio_c._1968.jpg" image_license: "CC BY-SA 4.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # Album Rock And Heartland Rock ## What it is Rock that treats the album as a statement about adulthood, labor, landscape, and public life. ## Canon anchors - Bruce Springsteen, Darkness on the Edge of Town - Neil Young, Tonight's the Night - Tom Petty, Wildflowers ## What succeeds - ordinary detail rendered with moral and melodic weight - band arrangements that feel lived-in - songs sequenced like a social or personal document ## What fails - adult-rock professionalism with no stakes - generic “heartland” imagery ## Use it when You need rock grounded in narrative realism and durable songcraft.

entries.title: British Invasion, Garage, And Proto-Punk

entries.markdown: --- image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/6f/Velvet_Crest_By_Popular_Demand_Band_Live_Performance_Mineral_City_Ohio_c._1968.jpg" image_alt: "Velvet Crest, a 1960s garage rock band from Mineral City, Ohio originally known as By Popular Demand, perform at a concert c. 1968." image_credit: "Imokurnotok" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3AVelvet_Crest_By_Popular_Demand_Band_Live_Performance_Mineral_City_Ohio_c._1968.jpg" image_license: "CC BY-SA 4.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # British Invasion, Garage, And Proto-Punk ## What it is Mid-1960s to early-1970s rock where riffs sharpen, songs get ruder, and band identity becomes everything. ## Canon anchors - The Kinks, The Kinks Are the Village Green Preservation Society - The Sonics, Here Are the Sonics - The Stooges, Fun House ## What succeeds - concise songs with abrasive personality - garage rawness used as force, not excuse - proto-punk tension inside still-catchy writing ## What fails - retro fuzz fetish - attitude with no song core ## Use it when You need raw band chemistry and anti-polish drive.

# Garage Revival And Rock Renewal

## What it is

Later returns to leaner rock-band language after periods of overproduction or genre exhaustion.

## Canon anchors

- The White Stripes, White Blood Cells
- The Strokes, Is This It
- Parquet Courts, Wide Awake!

## What succeeds

- stripping back to essentials without losing identity
- modern tension or wit inside classic band language
- recordings that preserve physical attack

## What fails

- retro-pastiche cool
- revival with no reason to exist now

## Use it when

You want to refresh band music by cutting excess instead of chasing novelty plugins.

# Hard Rock, Glam, And Arena Rock

## What it is

Large-scale riff music where confidence, hooks, and stage-sized drama are central.

## Canon anchors

- Led Zeppelin, Physical Graffiti
- Queen, A Night at the Opera
- AC/DC, Highway to Hell

## What succeeds

- riffs, grooves, and choruses all pulling equally hard
- spectacle that still feels dangerous or funny
- production that enlarges the band without sterilizing it

## What fails

- macho emptiness
- size without memorable writing

## Use it when

You need big physical payoff and theatrical attack.

entries.markdown: --- image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/6f/Velvet_Crest_By_Popular_Demand_Band_Live_Performance_Mineral_City_Ohio_c._1968.jpg" image_alt: "Velvet Crest, a 1960s garage rock band from Mineral City, Ohio originally known as By Popular Demand, perform at a concert c. 1968." image_credit: "Imokurnotok" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3AVelvet_Crest_By_Popular_Demand_Band_Live_Performance_Mineral_City_Ohio_c._1968.jpg" image_license: "CC BY-SA 4.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # Rock And Roll And Rockabilly ## What it is The early fusion of rhythm and blues, country, youth energy, and amplified drive into a concise pop form. ## Canon anchors - Chuck Berry, The Great Twenty-Eight - Little Richard, Here's Little Richard - Elvis Presley Sun sessions ## What succeeds - riffs and vocal charisma working immediately - dance momentum with minimal arrangement waste - attitude carried by rhythm, not only image ## What fails - retro recreation with no danger or joy - surface cool replacing rhythmic bite ## Use it when You need primal hook-writing and body-first momentum.

headings.text: Early rhythm and blues and foundational crossover
Motown, Northern soul, and polished propulsion
Quiet storm, contemporary R&B, and vocal luxury

entries.markdown:
--- image: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/c8/Jordan_Taylor_Hanson_%287991663965%29.jpg" image_alt: "

Hanson performs Enmore Theatre, Sydney, Australia

Tonight Hanson performed to a packed house at the famous Enmore Theatre.

The boys are performing down under live for the their first tour in seven years.

Hanson started writing Shout It Out while touring their 2007 disc, The Walk, and lead vocalist Taylor says it’s the most “holistic” and the least interfered-with of their seven releases.

“There’s no co-writing with other collaborators as writers, no other producers, we produced the record [ourselves].”

“It feels great to know that you’re able to have some connection that is lasting,” he says. “There is something that goes on with music and with audiences that is so special. With us, a lot of the fans that have stuck around [we] really respect it and admire that dedication, especially Australian fans. They’ve always been amongst the most active and the most engaged.”

Australian fans can look forward to additional tour dates based on how quickly shows sell out in selective cities. “Every show is different,” Taylor says. “We change it up, it’s going to feature some new music and it’s going to also highlight some songs from albums past.”

Tonight all the fans got value for money and by the look of things the band will continue to enjoy strong touring and CD sales in Australia.

Set List:

Where’s The Love

Thinking Bout Something

A Minuet Without You

And I Waited

Crazy Beautiful

Strong Enough to Break

Madeline

Wish That I Was There

With You In Your Dreams

Oh Darling – Beatles Cover

Cant Stop

Thinking of You

Penny and Me

Give a Little

MMMbop

Hey

Lost Without Each Other

Encore:

If Only


Promo...

Isaac, Taylor and Zac Hanson are household names in Australia and it’s no surprise – in 1997 one in ten Australian households owned a copy of the band’s smash album Middle Of Nowhere and the nation’s airwaves, hearts and minds resonated to the infectious sound of the band’s smash hit MMMBop. Now, after a 7-year absence, HANSON are back with a brand new album and tour that is sure to capture the attention and imagination of a nation that has always embraced the band as their own.

HANSON have been making music together for two decades. Fifteen years ago, their out-of-the-blue, soul-inspired brand of American pop-rock‘n’roll was introduced to the world. Unaffected by charts or fads, they’ve spent more than a decade building a community of fans connected to one another and fuelled by the energy and craftsmanship of three brothers and their music, earning praise for their songwriting, musicianship and dynamic live performances, with New York‘s Village Voice calling them the “Finest Straight-Up Rock Band In America.”

Biography...

Native sons of Tulsa, Oklahoma, HANSON has been making music together for nearly two decades. Thirteen years ago, their out-of-the-blue, soul-inspired brand of American pop-rock‘n’roll was introduced to the world. Unaffected by charts or fads, they’ve spent more than a decade building a community of fans connected to one another and fueled by the energy and craftsmanship of three brothers and their music. Their fifth studio album, Shout It Out, was released on June 8th 2010 on their label 3CG Records. They deliver a powerful group of soulful, melodic tunes that will leave you with a contagious sense of optimism for the future and welcomed reminiscence for American rock ‘n’ roll.

Shout It Out is a collection of finely crafted, R&B-flavored pop-rock, a homage to the music that inspired HANSON when they started out. The album produced and written exclusively by the band is augmented with special guests: Funk Brothers bassist Bob Babbitt, who played on some of Motown’s greatest hits, and horn arranger Jerry Hey, who worked with Michael Jackson, Quincy Jones, Earth, Wind and Fire and many more.

“The anatomy of our band is similar to a ‘70s rock band – white guys from the Midwest who grew up listening to ‘50s and ‘60s soul, who are mixing guitars, with vintage keys and melodic songs,” Taylor Hanson says. Adds Isaac, “We’ll always be a bit rootsy ‘cause it’s in the Oklahoma soil (the state is also home to artists like Bob Wills, Woody Guthrie and the versatile Leon Russell), but what we’ve never stopped aspiring to is the great songwriters and performers who struck a chord with us first, that early rock ‘n’ rock roll and soul music from Chuck Berry to Otis Redding. We’ve rediscovered that aspiration on this record. There’s a little more swing and a little more air.”

While Shout It Out looks back in time musically, it also represents forward movement for HANSON. “We love playing classic songs,” says Zac Hanson. “But we never sat down and wrote our version of what that sounds like until now.”

HANSON emotes such fresh energy on the new album that it’s easy to forget that this band has been around the block a few times. Multiple Grammy nominations (drummer Zac is the youngest nominated songwriter in history), years of headline shows everywhere from Carnegie Hall to the Hollywood Bowl and world-records for the loudest concert audience in history.

They’ve sold millions of records and reached a level of adoration that few bands experience, and have also faced career obstacles that would have broken many artist’s resolve. They waded through four years of struggle with a new corporate label made up of inherited executives in discord with the band, which eventually lead the band to leave the label (chronicled in the documentary film Strong Enough To Break) and launch their own independent record company, 3CG Records, in 2003. Amidst a crumbling industry, they built their own infrastructure around a DIY attitude and their commitment to their music and their fans. Their independent moves paid off, building a brand that has grown with successful independent albums, singles and tours, during a decade when music sales plummeted. In addition, launching alongside their 2007 album The Walk, they championed calls for action on humanitarian efforts, galvanizing tens of thousands of people around the world to participate in barefoot one-mile walks to fight HIV/AIDS and extreme poverty in sub-Saharan Africa.

Talking to the band about their forthcoming offering sheds light on how they find balance between these different elements and how they have emerged with this current direction. “The feeling I had when we were writing was that – as a band – you should be able to talk about serious subjects and share your passions [like fighting AIDS in Africa], but especially on this album, it was important to be able to share joy,” Taylor says. While The Walk carried a weighty message, in contrast the songs on Shout It Out, written mostly in hotel rooms and during soundchecks while touring The Walk were, according to Taylor, “A breath of fresh air during our hectic schedule”.

Shout It Out showcases not only the music of HANSON, but the many aspects that have allowed the band to survive and stay relevant. “The album is about being alive, and the contrasts we are faced with,” Taylor says. “We’ve come from a generation leaving the analog world and moving into an interconnected culture at risk of forgetting where it came from. This album tries to put together those pieces, but with a bit of melody to back it up.”

While the new album draws inspiration from the past, HANSON is looking to an exciting new era for the band. Their insatiable desire to innovate with technology - seen in their plans to incorporate mobile live streaming video both onstage and off - is building an even stronger fan connection. They’re committed to their live concert experience, with special events like their sold out five-night stand in NYC featuring each of their albums performed in their entirety, culminating with the debut of Shout It Out live. HANSON has established a reputation as successful independent artists who are comfortable at charting their own path, standing at the crossroads of the analog and digital worlds, and offering fans the best of both.

Websites

Hanson official website www.hanson.net

Enmore Theatre www.enmoretheatre.com.au

Eva Rinaldi Photography www.evarinaldi.com

Eva Rinaldi Photography Flickr www.flickr.com/evarinaldiphotography

Music News Australia

www.musicnewsaustralia.com" image_credit: "Eva Rinaldi" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3AJordan_Taylor_Hanson_(7991663965).jpg" image_license: "CC BY-SA 2.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # Soul, R&B, And Funk ## Core identity This family is built around groove, vocal expressivity, sensual precision, and the conversion of gospel and blues feeling into modern popular form. It matters because so much admired music depends on its rhythmic pocket, vocal grammar, and arrangement logic. ## Main subgenres ### Early rhythm and blues and foundational crossover Ray Charles, Ruth Brown, Sam Cooke, and other early architects bridge gospel, blues, and pop. This branch matters because it establishes how secular feeling can still carry sacred intensity. ### Motown, Northern soul, and polished propulsion The Supremes, Marvin Gaye, Stevie Wonder, the Temptations, and countless writers and session players define the branch where elegance, groove, and mass accessibility meet. Communities reward it for discipline without sterility. ### Southern soul and deep soul Otis Redding, Aretha Franklin, Al Green, Ann Peebles, and Stax or Hi-associated artists intensify grit, church cadence, and band feel. This branch succeeds when the performance sounds like release under pressure. ### Funk and P-Funk James Brown, Sly and the Family Stone, Parliament-Funkadelic, Prince, and related branches turn rhythm-section interlock into the main event. The strongest funk records feel engineered at the level of every subdivision. ### Quiet storm, contemporary R&B, and vocal luxury From late-1970s smoothness through 1990s vocal R&B and later mainstream mutations, this branch emphasizes texture, intimacy, and arrangement sheen. It works when seduction still has rhythmic and harmonic personality. ### Neo-soul and alternative R&B D'Angelo, Erykah Badu, Frank Ocean, Solange, SZA, and many others reconnect groove, lyric interiority, and adventurous harmony or production. Communities reward this branch when mood does not dissolve the song. ## What communities keep rewarding - pocket that feels human and exact - vocals with dynamic range and phrasing authority - sensuality backed by harmonic or rhythmic craft - arrangements that leave space for the groove to breathe ## What usually fails - anonymous luxury-sheen production - pseudo-retro soul without vocal conviction - over-layered contemporary R&B with no rhythmic center - funk reduced to a bass preset and a wah pedal ## Why this family still matters Soul, R&B, and funk remain central because they teach how to make groove, intimacy, and vocal force coexist.

Trace Repeat is a funk and soul band from Oakland, California." image_credit: "MuddyChateau" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3ACecilia_Silvestre_Photography.jpg" image_license: "CC BY-SA 4.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # Early R&B ## What it is Postwar rhythm music where blues grit, gospel phrasing, and pop accessibility start to merge. ## Canon anchors - Ray Charles, The Birth of Soul - Ruth Brown Atlantic sides - Sam Cooke early crossover recordings ## What succeeds - strong backbeat with vocal authority - secular emotion retaining gospel urgency - direct hooks with rhythmic punch ## What fails - polished revivalism without body - generic retro horn charts ## Use it when You need the hinge between blues, soul, and pop modernity.

# Motown And Northern Soul

## What it is

Tightly arranged soul emphasizing melody, momentum, and polished but highly disciplined groove.

## Canon anchors

- Marvin Gaye, What's Going On
- The Supremes, Where Did Our Love Go
- The Four Tops and Martha and the Vandellas singles

## What succeeds

- precision that still feels joyous
- hooks, rhythm, and vocal arrangement aligned perfectly
- emotional clarity without oversinging

## What fails

- imitation that captures gloss but not propulsion
- nostalgia packages with no rhythmic urgency

## Use it when

You need elegant groove engineering and universal melodic immediacy.

# Neo-Soul And Alternative R&B

## What it is

Recent soul-adjacent music reconnecting groove, personal writing, and adventurous harmony or production.

## Canon anchors

- D'Angelo, Voodoo
- Erykah Badu, Mama's Gun
- Frank Ocean, Blonde

## What succeeds

- looseness that still feels intentional
- interior language with rhythmic backbone
- modern textures that do not erase soul phrasing

## What fails

- hazy prestige music with no songs
- alternative branding used to excuse weak pocket

## Use it when

You want soul vocabulary inside a contemporary or experimental frame.

Cory Henry is performing alongside his band The Funk Apostles. The audience is seen closely surrounding the stage, illuminated by concert lights and mobile phones." image_credit: "FingerWiki" image_credit_url: "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3ACory_Henry_performing_live_at_Republic_club_in_Tbilisi_(2023).jpg" image_license: "CC BY-SA 4.0" image_license_url: "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0" image_usage: "permissive" --- # Quiet Storm And Contemporary R&B ## What it is Smooth, intimate vocal music built around texture, sensual pacing, and polished arrangement. ## Canon anchors - Sade, Love Deluxe - Janet Jackson, The Velvet Rope - Aaliyah, One in a Million ## What succeeds - restraint with a strong rhythmic center - lush production leaving space for the vocal - sensuality rendered with detail, not cliché ## What fails - anonymous luxury sheen - mood replacing songwriting ## Use it when You need intimacy, nocturnal pacing, and modern vocal framing.

# Southern And Deep Soul

## What it is

Soul music with church grit, room-feel band dynamics, and high emotional temperature.

## Canon anchors

- Aretha Franklin, I Never Loved a Man the Way I Love You
- Otis Redding, Otis Blue
- Al Green, Call Me

## What succeeds

- vocal grain preserved rather than sanitized
- band interplay serving release and ache
- understatement used as power

## What fails

- faux-grit production
- retro soul without vocal stakes

## Use it when

You need maximum emotional presence from relatively spare means.

# Instrument Reference

## Scope

This folder covers the major instruments and instrument families that matter most across modern music history.

The goal is not organology for its own sake. The goal is to explain how instruments actually function in successful and unsuccessful records.

## What Each File Covers

- the instrument or family in musical terms
- what it is especially good at expressing
- how it has been used in major genres
- what successful use sounds like
- what failed or weak use sounds like

## Core Rule

Instruments do not carry meaning by themselves. Their meaning comes from register, timing, arrangement, performance technique, recording method, and cultural context.

The same instrument can feel intimate, brutal, comic, sacred, fake, or exhausted depending on how it is written, played, and recorded.

# Bass

## Role

Bass binds rhythm to harmony. It can stabilize a song, destabilize it, sensualize it, or make the whole track feel dangerous.

## Successful uses

- lines that converse with drums instead of merely shadowing roots
- register control that opens or tightens the track
- repetition used to hypnotize, not deaden
- melodic bass writing that quietly redirects emotion

## Failed uses

- sub-only mud with no articulation
- overbusy playing that competes with everything
- bass as an afterthought in arrangements that need weight
- sterile DI tone with no character where character is needed

## Key lesson

Bass succeeds when it changes the body’s relationship to the song.

# Brass

## Role

Brass instruments project force, ceremony, irony, celebration, alarm, and attack. In ensemble music they can be architectural or disruptive.

## Successful uses

- punchy arranging that clarifies rhythm and accent
- brass lines used for tension, satire, or grandeur
- dynamic placement that avoids constant blare
- voicings that let trombone, trumpet, and low brass play distinct dramatic roles

## Failed uses

- triumphant cliché
- horn stabs used as empty emphasis marks
- overscored sections that feel like generic uplift
- brass forced into mixes with no room to breathe

## Key lesson

Brass works when impact is shaped, not permanent.

# Drums And Percussion

## Role

Drums organize time, body, and intensity. Percussion can mark ritual, propulsion, swing, violence, release, play, or dread depending on pattern and sound.

## Successful uses

- groove that pushes and breathes
- drum tones chosen for function, not just size
- percussion used to create social space and movement
- dynamic control that lets sections change weight and meaning

## Failed uses

- quantized rigidity with no aesthetic reason
- overcompression that erases movement
- “epic” drums pasted onto weak music
- busy fills that substitute for structure

## Key lesson

Great drumming is not only precision. It is time-feel and dramatic judgment.

# Guitar

## Role

The guitar is unusually adaptable. It can deliver harmony, riff, rhythm, noise, ornament, melody, and texture. Acoustic and electric guitars are effectively different emotional machines.

## Historical uses

It moves from amateur accompaniment and folk song into blues, jazz rhythm work, country picking, rock amplification, metal distortion, ambient textural work, and experimental prepared or processed sound.

## Successful uses

- riff writing with contour and force
- rhythmic guitar that creates real momentum
- electric tone chosen as part of composition
- acoustic detail used for intimacy and image-making

## Failed uses

- generic indie strumming
- guitar hero solos with no dramatic consequence
- distorted walls that hide weak harmonic or rhythmic content
- nostalgia use that quotes old eras without transformation

## Key lesson

Guitar wins when touch and tone carry meaning, not just notes.

# Piano And Keyboards

## Role

Piano is one of the clearest instruments for harmony, register contrast, and compositional architecture. Keyboards more broadly extend that into organ, electric piano, clavinet, and digital timbral control.

## Successful uses

- voicings that shape emotional color instead of stating chords bluntly
- rhythmic comping that deepens groove
- left-hand design that changes harmonic gravity
- keyboard choice used as a narrative decision: grand piano, upright, Rhodes, organ, synth-key patch

## Failed uses

- prestige piano ballad clichés
- overplayed accompaniment that leaves no space
- digital keyboard tones chosen for convenience rather than identity
- sentimentality with no harmonic personality

## Key lesson

Keyboard instruments succeed when they do more than “fill in” harmony. They need to define space and mood.

# Strings

## Role

Strings can create warmth, tension, glide, collective swell, chamber intimacy, or cinematic dread. They are often used to imply sophistication, but their best use is structural, not ornamental.

## Successful uses

- arranging strings as independent voices, not wallpaper
- using bow pressure, articulation, and register for emotional specificity
- integrating strings with rhythm sections or electronics instead of just laying them on top
- chamber writing that acts like counterpoint or dramatic narration

## Failed uses

- syrupy prestige padding
- “cinematic” swelling that says nothing new
- strings that flatten rhythmic energy
- over-romanticization of songs that need edges, not varnish

## Key lesson

Strings are powerful when they act as characters in the arrangement.

# Studio As Instrument

## Role

From tape editing to multitracking to DAWs, the studio became an instrument in its own right. Mic choice, room sound, editing, layering, distortion, spatial design, and automation all shape musical meaning.

## Successful uses

- recording decisions that change the emotional reading of performance
- edits that create form rather than simply fix mistakes
- room and space used dramatically
- production that sounds inseparable from the composition

## Failed uses

- overcorrection that removes human risk
- expensive neutrality
- cinematic reverb and polish used as a shortcut to importance
- editing that hides weak writing rather than sharpening strong material

## Key lesson

Modern music often succeeds or fails at the production level before the listener can even explain why.

# Synths, Drum Machines, And Samplers

## Role

These tools made timbre, sequence, and repetition central compositional materials. They can produce impossible sounds, exact patterns, altered time-feel, and new forms of authorship.

## Successful uses

- patch design that becomes part of the identity of the piece
- drum-machine patterns used with groove consciousness rather than robotic default
- sampling that reframes memory, history, or texture
- hybrids where electronic tools and acoustic sources intensify each other

## Failed uses

- preset dependency
- flattened quantization with no swing or concept
- sample use that feels purely referential
- machine precision that removes tension instead of redefining it

## Key lesson

Electronic tools succeed when the artist makes them speak personally.

# Turntables And DJ Tools

## Role

Turntables, mixers, and DJ tools changed music by turning playback into performance and editing into live composition.

## Successful uses

- break extension and transitions that reshape time
- scratching used rhythmically and texturally
- sequencing records to build social movement and tension
- sampling logic that treats records as raw material rather than sacred objects

## Failed uses

- flashy turntablism with no musical arc
- DJ sets that are only playlist management
- sample collage with no new structure
- nostalgia about crate-digging that forgets the compositional point

## Key lesson

DJ tools matter because they turned selection, repetition, and interruption into composition.

# Voice

## Role

The voice is the fastest path from sound to personhood. Even when lyrics are abstract, phrasing, breath, accent, register, and strain tell the listener how to hear the world.

## Successful uses

- phrasing that shapes rhythm rather than sitting on top of it
- imperfection used expressively
- timbral identity that makes a singer recognizable within seconds
- harmonies used to widen emotion, not just sweeten the surface

## Failed uses

- excessive tuning that erases the body without creating a new aesthetic payoff
- empty melisma
- generic “indie” murmuring or prestige whispering with no textual precision
- talk-singing with no dramatic purpose

## Key lesson

The voice succeeds when it sounds necessary, not merely competent.

# Woodwinds And Reeds

## Role

Saxophone, clarinet, flute, oboe, bassoon, and related reeds bring breath, line, vulnerability, and attack in ways that differ sharply from brass or strings.

## Successful uses

- saxophone or clarinet as a second voice rather than a solo cliché
- flute and high reeds used for color without prettiness by default
- woodwind writing that deepens chamber texture or urban unease
- improvisation and phrasing that foreground breath and grain

## Failed uses

- smooth-jazz dilution
- faux sophistication via background reed pads
- overuse of saxophone as an instant nostalgia signal
- woodwind parts written without regard for phrasing or breath

## Key lesson

Reeds matter when their physicality remains audible.
